textPlain: vim +'exec "norm 99o"|%s/$/\=line(".")/|vert new|exec "norm i^M^MFizz^M^MBuzz^MFizz^M^M^MFizz^MBuzz^M^MFizz^M^M^MFizzBuzz"|exec "norm gg^VG$y"|bd!|let @q="A    ^[\"0gpj"|exec "norm gg10@q"|silent /100/+,$d|silent %s/\d\+\s\+\(\w\+\)/\1'Now I see it's the same solution as in the post.

I always wanted to write this with duff's device. switch with fall through is almost never a good thing but it allows for some 'interesting' tricks.  Wouldn't be hard, but I have kids so finding half an hour to concentrate is hard.

No matter how you calculate FizzBuzz, it is bad engineering.  const Fizzbuzz = "1 2. Fizz, 4 ... "
  Print Fizzbuzz

Much like stop50's solution, I also used the modulo, but I make use of the terminal to overwrite the number. It's only three lines of code, but I split up the list to be more readable on here.This works from 1 to 100000000000000000000 before it overflows, and 100000000000000000000 is above the max size of a unsigned 64 bit int, so I feel that it's good enough    fizzbuzz = [
        "fizzbuzz            ",
        "", "",
        "fizz                ",
        "",
        "buzz                ",
        "fizz                ",
        "", "",
        "fizz                ",
        "buzz                ",
        "",
        "fizz                ",
        "", "" ]
    for n in range(99999999999999999999-30, 100000000000000000000):
        print(f"{n}\r{fizzbuzz[n%15]}")
Answer: Modulo or adding "%3" and "%5" before masking it
Yeah, you can even generate a template for any length of number prefix too. What’s the point of the game without booleans?
